title: Live Debugger — Bug Hunting Fundamentals
description: Validate your understanding of the Dynatrace Live Debugger and the three bugs you hunted: non-breaking breakpoints, snapshots, watching variables, version-control integration, and on-agent data masking.
imagine: You just finished hunting three production bugs in the TODO app with the Dynatrace Live Debugger — without a single redeploy-to-debug cycle. Now confirm you understood how the pieces fit together.
yourGoal: Answer six questions about the Live Debugger and the bugs you fixed in this lab.

story.introduction: The lab ran a TODO app on a Dynatrace-monitored k3d cluster with three bugs. You navigated from cluster → traces/logs → code, set non-breaking breakpoints, and read snapshots to find each root cause.
story.context: Everything you need to answer is in the lab pages you just completed.

questions.title: What does a non-breaking breakpoint do?
questions.content: Throughout the lab you set **non-breaking** breakpoints in a running production app. What happens when one is hit?

options.text: Dynatrace captures a snapshot of the variables at that line without pausing or slowing the request
options.explanation: Correct! A non-breaking breakpoint captures state and lets execution continue — safe to use in production.

options.text: The thread is paused until a developer resumes it, like a classic IDE breakpoint
options.explanation: That would be a breaking breakpoint and is unsafe in production. Live Debugger breakpoints are non-breaking.

options.text: The pod is restarted to attach a debugger
options.explanation: No restart or redeploy is needed — that is the whole point of the Live Debugger.

options.text: Extra logging code is injected into the source and redeployed
options.explanation: No code changes or redeploys happen; the agent captures the snapshot at runtime.

questions.explanation: The Live Debugger captures a snapshot of in-scope variables at the breakpoint and execution continues uninterrupted — debugging production without stopping it.
hints: Think about why it is safe to set one in production.
The request still returned normally while you captured data.

questions.title: Bug 1 — why did 'Clear completed' do nothing?
questions.content: The `clearCompletedTodos` snapshot showed `todos` with length 3 and `todoStore` with length 0. What was the root cause?

options.text: `remove` was called on `todoStore`, a freshly instantiated empty list, instead of the persistence-backed `todos`
options.explanation: Correct! The handler mutated the wrong, empty collection, so nothing was ever removed — yet it still returned 200.

options.text: The DELETE request was being blocked by the ingress
options.explanation: The request reached the handler and returned 200 — the bug was in the code, not the network.

options.text: The database connection had timed out
options.explanation: There was no failure; the wrong in-memory list was used.

options.text: The todos were completed but not marked completed
options.explanation: They were marked completed; the removal targeted the wrong list.

questions.explanation: `todoStore` was a new empty list. Removing from it never touched the real `todos`, so completed items survived while the request still succeeded with HTTP 200.
hints: Compare the two list lengths in the snapshot.
Which variable does the persistence layer actually back?

questions.title: Bug 2 — why were special characters stripped?
questions.content: Watching `newTodoRecord.title` across two snapshots showed it intact before line 26 and stripped after. What caused it?

options.text: A leftover `replaceAll("[^a-zA-Z0-9\\s]+", "")` sanitisation rewrote the title before storing it
options.explanation: Correct! The regex removed every non-alphanumeric character. Commenting it out preserves the original title.

options.text: The browser stripped the characters before sending the request
options.explanation: The first snapshot proved the title arrived intact server-side.

options.text: Grail removed the characters during log ingestion
options.explanation: Log ingestion does not modify application variables; the change was in the code.

options.text: The title column had a length limit
options.explanation: Characters were removed by pattern, not truncated by length.

questions.explanation: A regex `replaceAll` sanitisation stripped all non-alphanumeric characters. Watching the variable before and after the line pinpointed exactly where the mutation happened.
hints: The bug sat between your two breakpoints.
Watch the title variable change across snapshots.

questions.title: Bug 3 — why was the duplicate malformed?
questions.content: In `duplicateTodo`, the new record's title and ID appeared transposed. What was wrong?

options.text: The `setId` and `setTitle` arguments were swapped — the ID got the old title and the title got a new UUID
options.explanation: Correct! Swapping the two setter arguments back keeps the title and assigns a fresh unique ID.

options.text: The duplicate endpoint returned a 500 error
options.explanation: The trace showed HTTP 200; the values were just assigned to the wrong fields.

options.text: Two tasks shared the same primary key
options.explanation: The ID was being set to the title, not duplicated; the fix generates a new UUID.

options.text: The map iteration order was non-deterministic
options.explanation: The snapshot showed swapped values, not an ordering issue.

questions.explanation: The setters were crossed: the ID was set to the title and the title to a new UUID. The duplicate must keep the title and receive a new unique ID for the persistence layer.
hints: Look at the map items [0] and [1] in the snapshot.
Which field needs a brand-new unique value?

questions.title: Where does Live Debugger data masking happen?
questions.content: You added a rule to mask the `title` variable. Where is the masking applied?

options.text: On the OneAgent, so the sensitive value never leaves the host or reaches Dynatrace servers
options.explanation: Correct! Masking happens at the source on the OneAgent — masked data is never transmitted or stored by Dynatrace.

options.text: In the browser after the snapshot is downloaded
options.explanation: By then the value would already have been transmitted; masking happens earlier, on the agent.

options.text: In Grail during storage, after ingestion
options.explanation: Masking is applied before transmission, on the OneAgent — not at storage time.

options.text: Only when you export the snapshot to a file
options.explanation: Masking is always applied at capture time on the agent, not at export.

questions.explanation: Live Debugger masking runs on the OneAgent. Sensitive values are redacted at the source and are never sent to or stored on Dynatrace servers — and rules apply on the fly, with no restart.
hints: Think about where the snapshot data originates.
The lab stressed that masked data never leaves the host.

questions.title: How does the Live Debugger know which source code to show?
questions.content: The `solve_bugN` functions set `DT_LIVEDEBUGGER_COMMIT` and `DT_LIVEDEBUGGER_REMOTE_ORIGIN` on the deployment. What do these enable?

options.text: They tell the Live Debugger the exact git remote and commit a deployment is running, so it fetches the matching source automatically
options.explanation: Correct! Setting these env vars in your CI/CD makes version-control resolution automatic and accurate per environment.

options.text: They authenticate the OneAgent to your Dynatrace tenant
options.explanation: Tenant auth uses tokens; these variables are about source-code resolution.

options.text: They enable the Live Debugger ActiveGate module
options.explanation: The ActiveGate module is enabled via the DynaKube capability, not these variables.

options.text: They control which Kubernetes namespace is monitored
options.explanation: Namespace selection is done with breakpoint filters, not these variables.

questions.explanation: `DT_LIVEDEBUGGER_REMOTE_ORIGIN` and `DT_LIVEDEBUGGER_COMMIT` pin a deployment to an exact repo and revision. Setting them in CI/CD lets the Live Debugger fetch the right source for every environment automatically.
hints: Think about how the debugger maps a running pod to a line of code.
These are set per deployment in CI/CD.

learningObjectives: Explain what a non-breaking breakpoint captures and why it is production-safe
Diagnose a logic bug that returns HTTP 200 but mutates the wrong data
Use watched variables across snapshots to locate where a value changes
Identify the root cause of swapped setter arguments
Explain where Live Debugger data masking is applied
Describe how version-control environment variables resolve source code per deployment
